tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Investigate the legal precedents that established the 'first sale' doctrine, a principle crucial for the existence of the video rental industry (e.g., Blockbuster, Redbox). First, identify the 1984 Supreme Court case where a motion picture industry leader famously compared the VCR to the 'Boston Strangler.' Second, identify the 2013 Supreme Court case involving a Thai student that applied similar principles to imported goods. For both cases, provide the official case name, the exact Supreme Court vote tally, and the specific economic threat or market consequence the plaintiffs argued would result if the court ruled against them.

Identify the Cornell graduate student whose 2013 Supreme Court victory regarding the importation of textbooks reaffirmed the 'First Sale' doctrine—the same legal principle that historically enabled the existence of the video rental industry. Then, examining the industry response to this legal boundary in the digital realm during the same period, describe the specific technical mechanisms for a 'digital secondhand market' outlined in patent filings by Amazon and Apple in early 2013. Finally, name the Massachusetts-based startup that was simultaneously entangled in litigation for attempting to operate a marketplace for pre-owned digital music files.

source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The 1984 Supreme Court case was **Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.**, decided by a 5-4 vote. Jack Valenti, president of the Motion Picture Association of America, famously testified to Congress that "the VCR is to the American film producer and the American public as the Boston Strangler is to the woman home alone." Universal Studios argued that widespread VCR adoption would devastate the film industry by enabling mass copyright infringement, potentially destroying theatrical revenues and eliminating the incentive to produce new content. They claimed home recording would undermine the entire economic foundation of Hollywood.

The 2013 case was **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.**, decided 6-3. Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai student, imported foreign-edition textbooks purchased abroad and resold them in the United States. Wiley argued that allowing such imports would decimate the publishing industry's ability to engage in geographic price discrimination, potentially causing publishers to raise prices globally or cease publishing in developing markets altogether. They warned this would destroy the $8 billion educational publishing market and eliminate affordable textbooks in poorer countries.

Both cases affirmed the first sale doctrine's broad application. Sony established that technology enabling private copying could be legal if it had substantial non-infringing uses. Kirtsaeng extended first sale protection to lawfully made copies manufactured abroad. These rulings proved foundational for video rental businesses, used book stores, and secondary markets generally, allowing purchasers to rent, lend, or resell lawfully acquired copies without copyright holder permission.

## References

[1] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984)
[2] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)
[3] Congressional testimony of Jack Valenti, House Judiciary Committee (1982)
[4] Brief for Respondent, Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., No. 11-697 (2012)
rounds.answer_b:
The 1984 case is **Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984)**, the “Betamax case,” decided **5–4** in Sony’s favor.[3][4][5] In the surrounding policy fight, MPAA president **Jack Valenti** warned Congress that “the VCR is to the American film producer and the American public as the Boston Strangler is to the woman home alone,” capturing Hollywood’s claim that home recording would “destroy” the motion picture industry.[1][6] In the litigation, Universal and Disney argued that if Sony were allowed to sell VCRs without liability, **unauthorized home taping and library-building would so erode theatrical and broadcast markets that studios would lose control over distribution and suffer massive revenue loss**, effectively threatening the economic viability of future film and TV production.[2][4][5]

The 2013 case is **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)**, decided **6–3** for Kirtsaeng. Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai student, imported cheaper foreign editions of Wiley textbooks and resold them in the U.S.; Wiley claimed this violated copyright. Wiley argued that if the first-sale doctrine applied to **copies manufactured abroad**, publishers would face **widespread parallel importation and gray‑market arbitrage**, undermining territorial pricing, collapsing the ability to charge higher U.S. prices, and thereby **threatening the business model that funded textbook development and other copyrighted works**.

## References
[1] Sound & Vision – Flashback 1984: Supreme Court Upholds Right to Tape  
[2] Litman, “The Story of Sony v. Universal Studios” (Mich. L. Rev. book chapter)  
[3] Wikipedia – Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.  
[4] Justia – Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, 464 U.S. 417 (1984)  
[5] Marquette Law School Faculty Blog – “The Most Important Supreme Court Case in Copyright Law”  
[6] R Street – “The Supreme Court case that will decide the future of television”  
 Justia – Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)  
 Wikipedia – Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.  
 SCOTUSblog – Case argument/analysis for Kirtsaeng v. Wiley
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the entities, vote counts, and arguments, satisfying the core checklist. However, Agent A is the clear winner on User Experience and Formatting. 

1. **Citations**: Agent A provides a clean, properly numbered reference list. Agent B's reference list is broken (the last three sources are unnumbered) and it fails to include inline citations for the entire second paragraph (the Kirtsaeng case).
2. **Synthesis**: Agent A includes a concluding paragraph that ties the cases back to the user's prompt regarding the 'video rental industry' and the 'first sale doctrine,' providing better context. Agent B simply lists the two cases without that connective tissue.
3. **Readability**: Agent A's formatting is cleaner. Agent B's bolding is acceptable, but the broken citation mechanics make it feel like a lower-quality product.

Supap Kirtsaeng, the Cornell graduate student who prevailed in the 2013 Supreme Court case, fundamentally reaffirmed that the First Sale doctrine applies to goods lawfully manufactured abroad [1]. His victory meant that physical copies of copyrighted works, including textbooks purchased overseas, could be resold in the United States without permission from copyright holders.

Coincidentally, in early 2013, both Amazon and Apple filed patents exploring digital secondhand marketplaces that would circumvent First Sale limitations in the digital realm [2][3]. Amazon's patent application (published January 2013) described a system where users could transfer "used" digital objects through an electronic marketplace. The mechanism involved deleting the digital file from the seller's storage devices while simultaneously transferring it to the buyer, with the system maintaining a usage history that could affect pricing—essentially creating artificial scarcity and wear for digital goods [2]. Apple's patent filing from March 2013 outlined similar functionality, proposing a system where digital content could be given a limited number of transfers, with each subsequent sale potentially degrading quality or features to simulate physical deterioration [3].

During this same period, ReDigi, a Massachusetts-based startup, found itself in federal court defending its attempt to create a marketplace for "pre-owned" digital music files [4]. ReDigi's technology claimed to verify that sellers' files were legally purchased from iTunes, then deleted the original while transferring it to buyers. However, Capitol Records sued ReDigi for copyright infringement, arguing that any reproduction of copyrighted files—even in the transfer process—violated their exclusive rights [4][5]. The court ultimately ruled against ReDigi in 2013, finding that the First Sale doctrine did not apply to digital goods that required reproduction for transfer, effectively blocking the digital resale market that Amazon and Apple were simultaneously exploring through patents [5].

## References

[1] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013)
[2] Amazon Technologies, Inc., U.S. Patent Application No. 20130060616 A1 (published Jan. 29, 2013)
[3] Apple Inc., U.S. Patent Application No. 20130073387 A1 (filed March 2013)
[4] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013)
[5] United States District Court Southern District of New York, Case No. 12-cv-0095 (2013)
rounds.answer_b:
The Cornell graduate student was **Supap Kirtsaeng**, whose 2013 Supreme Court win in **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.** confirmed that the **first‑sale doctrine applies to lawfully made copies manufactured abroad**, thereby protecting his right to resell imported textbooks.[1]

In response to this clarified boundary in copyright exhaustion, major platforms simultaneously explored how to implement a **“digital secondhand” market** that would mimic resale while maintaining technical control:

- **Amazon’s early‑2013 patent** (often described as covering an “electronic marketplace for used digital objects”) proposed a system where a user could **transfer a digital item (e.g., an e‑book, audio file, or video) from one user account to another**, while the system automatically **deletes or disables access in the seller’s account upon transfer**, enforcing *one copy per license* through server‑side controls and usage records.[2][3] The architecture contemplated **queues, access rights management, and metadata tracking** to ensure that each resale was effectively a reassignment rather than duplication.[2][3]

- **Apple’s 2013 “used digital content” patent application** similarly described a marketplace where **ownership of a digital good is reassigned at the account level**, with **digital rights management (DRM) and a transaction log** ensuring that only the buyer retains playable access after the transaction.[4] Apple’s design relied on **unique identifiers and authorization checks** so that playback devices would refuse content no longer associated with the seller’s account.[4]

During this same period, the Massachusetts‑based startup **ReDigi Inc.**, headquartered in Cambridge, was embroiled in high‑profile litigation with Capitol Records for operating an online marketplace that allowed users to trade **pre‑owned digital music files**, a model the label claimed infringed reproduction and distribution rights despite ReDigi’s “transfer‑not‑copy” technical claims.[5][6]

## References  
[1] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013), Justia/Wikipedia  
[2] U.S. Patent filings on Amazon “secondary market for digital objects” (2013 news coverage)  
[3] Tech/press analyses of Amazon’s “used digital content” patent  
[4] Reports on Apple’s 2013 “used digital content” / digital resale patent application  
[5] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., S.D.N.Y. opinions and case summaries  
[6] News coverage of ReDigi, Cambridge, MA–based digital music resale startup and litigation
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the core entities (Supap Kirtsaeng, ReDigi) and the relevant patents (Amazon, Apple). However, Agent A is superior in terms of **utility and rigor**. 

1. **Citation Quality**: Agent A provided specific patent application numbers (e.g., 20130060616 A1) and proper legal citations (e.g., 568 U.S. 519). Agent B provided vague, unprofessional placeholders in its reference list (e.g., "Justia/Wikipedia", "Tech/press analyses"), which significantly reduces trust and utility for a user researching legal/technical topics.
2. **Specificity**: Agent A included the specific patent numbers in the text, allowing for immediate verification, whereas Agent B only gave general descriptions.
3. **Narrative**: Agent A wove the legal victory, the industry's patent response, and the startup's litigation into a cohesive narrative that explained the *relationship* between the events (circumventing the physical First Sale doctrine). 

Agent B had better visual formatting (bullet points), but the lack of citation quality makes it the weaker response for a detailed research query.
